entry.title: Re: Automatic batch determination with releasing the prod...
entry.content:
If your requirement is to validate or enforce batch determination for components at release, here are the practical options:

  • Keep Batch Entry = 3. Use business process controls or flexible workflow to prevent order release completion unless batches are assigned to all components. This ensures compliance without automation.
  • Build an extension that listens to the production order release event. The extension can call the batch determination logic (via API or service) and update the order’s component list with assigned batches. This mimics value 4 behavior but outside the core system, keeping Clean Core intact.
  • But please note, SAP recommends performing batch determination at goods issue for components. This ensures that the actual available batches are considered at the point of consumption, avoiding mismatches between planned and actual inventory. It is the standard approach in Public Cloud.

entry.title: Re: Guidance Needed: SAP Learning Path for Procurement &...
entry.content:
Hello @uday_0-14 ,

I think a good starting point is Exploring SAP Cloud ERP. This is relevant for both public cloud and private cloud.

 

The decision on whether you should focus on Public Edition, Private Edition, or both is a very personal one and probably depends on where you see your career in the next few years (consulting, in-house, key user, …) and also which system(s) your employer will work with.

For me personally, I see public cloud as the future and that’s where I focus my efforts on 😊

 

After completing above-mentioned entry point, my advice would be to start with the more general Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and get comfortable here before focusing Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Sourcing and Procurement.

Alternatively, for Private Edition you may start with Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Sourcing and Procurement.

 

For each Learning Journey, SAP provides some information about the recommended prerequisites, like here:

 

Helpful links for you:

  • Exploring SAP Cloud ERP
  • Cloud Onboarding for SAP Cloud ERP
  • Getting Started with Support from SAP (Support Accreditation)
  • Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Get Certified: Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Publ
  • Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, Sourcing and Procu
  • Get Certified: Implementation Consultant - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Publ
  • Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Sourcing and Proc
  • Get Certified: SAP Certified - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition,

 

For each Learning Journey that starts with "Implementing..." (links above), you can scroll to the very bottom to find:

  • Access to a practice system including a PDF with training exercises
  • Live Sessions in which topics from the learning journey are explained in more detail
  • Links to planned "Get Certified" Sessions, which will cover all content relevant for a Certification

 

I hope this helps.

Best regards & good luck on your journey,

Kerisha

entry.title: Best Practices for JWT Validation and JWKS Caching in SAP API Management using KVM
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
I’m designing a JWT validation flow in SAP API Management for an integration project. The goal is to keep the proxy environment‑agnostic while ensuring high performance through JWKS caching. I’d appreciate feedback on the approach and any potential improvements.

Current Flow Summary:

  1. Validate the request body to ensure it is well‑formed JSON.
  2. Remove sensitive headers (e.g., Cookies) before further processing.
  3. Read the IdP base URL from an environment‑specific KVM.
  4. Normalize the URL using JavaScript for service callouts.
  5. Look up JWKS in cache.
  6. Call the IdP’s .well-known/jwks.json endpoint if the cache is empty.
  7. Cache the JWKS (TTL: 120 seconds).
  8. Verify JWT (RS256, scopes).
  9. Validate the issuer claim against the KVM value.

    Are there alternative or recommended ways to further externalize configuration (beyond KVMs) while keeping the proxy fully environment‑agnostic?

    Looking forward to your insights on optimizing this zero‑touch setup.

    Best regards,
    Somya

entry.title: Re: Guidance Needed: SAP Learning Path for Procurement &...
entry.content:
Beginner Level

  • S4H00 – SAP S/4HANA Overview: Introductory course for learners new to SAP.
  • S4500 – Business Processes in SAP S/4HANA Sourcing and Procurement: Comprehensive overview of procurement processes.


Intermediate Level

  • S4PR1 – Procurement in SAP S/4HANA: Covers purchase requisitions, purchase orders, contracts, and supplier management.
  • S4PR2 – Advanced Procurement in SAP S/4HANA: Focuses on configuration, flexible workflows, and integration with other modules.
  • Hands-on labs: Practice configuring enterprise structures, source lists, and info records.


Advanced Level

  • Specialized courses on Inventory Management, Consumption-Based Planning, and Analytics.
  • S4H400 – Embedded Analytics Foundation: Learn how to use real-time reporting in procurement.
  • Project-based training: Configure end-to-end procure-to-pay (P2P) scenarios.


Certification Path

Target C_TS452 – SAP Certified Application Associate – SAP S/4HANA Sourcing and Procurement.

Prep with:

  • SAP Learning Hub subscription (official practice exams, e-learning).
  • Udemy/Edoxi courses aligned with C_TS452 exam blueprint.
  • Practice tests and case-based scenarios

Below would be a 12‑month structured roadmap you can follow to go from beginner → job‑ready → certified in SAP S/4HANA Sourcing & Procurement. It balances theory, practice, and certification prep so you steadily build confidence.

I suggest you to try modifying the parameters controlling timeout:

https://me.sap.com/notes/2735916

webgui: rdisp/gui_auto_logout note 3329632 Timeout for ITS/WebGUI sessions

https://me.sap.com/notes/3329632

I also notice that the host in url is "fioriqa.adds.nwnatural.com". Can you confirm whether you are using web dispatcher?

If yes, please also modify the timeout parameter for web dispatcher:

1757252 How to analyse problems related to session loss, logoff or blank screens caused by timeout issues
https://me.sap.com/notes/1757252

Also the following SAP Note was released at Kernel Patch :

3639412 - Backend error after textfield input https://me.sap.com/notes/0003639412
To resolve the issue, kindly update your Kernel to latest version.

2221289 - How to install package sapwebgui https://me.sap.com/notes/2221289
3628821 - FAQ on Patching SAP Kernel https://me.sap.com/notes/3628821

After you implement SAP Note 3639412, please make sure to also implement the SAP Notes below:

3667332 - Long text issue after upgrade SAP kernel https://me.sap.com/notes/3667332
3674227 - Backend error after textedit input with popup https://me.sap.com/notes/3667332

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: SAP SRM On-Premise Transition: Context, Timelines, Successors, and What to Do Next
entry.content:
SAP Supplier Relationship Management (SRM) is part of SAP Business Suite 7 and has long been the backbone for strategic and operational procurement—covering sourcing, supplier collaboration, requisitioning, purchasing, and contracts—integrated with SAP ERP (ECC) and, more recently, S/4HANA back ends. Many organizations adopted SRM to standardize processes, enforce compliance, and drive savings through centralization and automation. 

 SAP is shifting focus from SAP SRM to align with its strategic move to cloud-based offerings and the S/4HANA platform. In 2020, SAP announced mainstream maintenance for Business Suite 7 through the end of 2027, with optional extended maintenance through the end of 2030. SAP SRM follows this policy, guiding customers toward modern successors that deliver faster innovation, better user experiences, embedded analytics, and networked supplier collaboration. See SAP Note 2881788 and SAP’s “SAP Extends SAP S/4HANA Maintenance Through 2040” announcement for context. 

Supported SAP SRM releases from Q1 2026: 

SAP will provide mainstream maintenance for the following SAP Supplier Relationship Management (SRM) Enhancement Packages until December 31, 2027 and provide optional extended maintenance from January 1, 2028 until December 31, 2030: 

- EHP2 for SAP SRM 7.0 (release level 7.02): support non-Unicode and Unicode deployments. 

- EHP3 for SAP SRM 7.0 (release level 7.13): support non-Unicode and Unicode deployments. 

- EHP4 for SAP SRM 7.0 (release level 7.14): support Unicode deployments.  

Note: If you run non-Unicode on EHP2/EHP3, consider Unicode migration as part of risk mitigation and transition planning.

Maintenance timeline for SAP SRM and what each phase means: 

SAP will provide mainstream maintenance for the latest three EHPs only i.e. EHP4, EHP3 and EHP2 only. Maintenance timeline for SAP Business Suite 7 which also includes SAP SRM are as below. 

- Until December 31, 2027: Mainstream maintenance with full standard support for supported SAP SRM releases. 

- From January 1, 2028 (default): Customer-specific maintenance with limited support (no new innovations, constrained legal/regulatory coverage); SAP does not support customer-developed extensions in this phase. 

- Optional January 1, 2028 to December 31, 2030: Extended maintenance for an additional fee; applies to the entire SAP Business Suite 7 core applications landscape and related add-ons (not selectable for subsets). 

- From January 1, 2031: Only customer-specific maintenance is available.

Please be aware, extended maintenance is not available for a subset of the customer installation only. It always applies to the full landscape of SAP Business Suite 7 core applications and all respective SAP Business Suite 7 Add-Ons. 

After its mainstream maintenance periods end in 2027 SAP SRM will no longer receive full standard support from SAP, though it will enter a limited "customer-specific maintenance" phase. 

Please check out SAP Note 52505 for general details regarding the maintenance phases of SAP applications and specifically customer-specific maintenance. 

Successor options: 

SAP S/4HANA for Central Procurement: Centralizes operational procurement across ECC and/or S/4HANA with native ERP integration and embedded analytics—ideal for harmonizing processes and supporting a broader S/4HANA journey. 

SAP Ariba: Cloud-based suite for strategic sourcing, supplier management, contracts, guided buying, and multi-enterprise collaboration—suited to organizations prioritizing rapid cloud innovation and supplier network scale. 

Hybrid: Pair S/4HANA Central Procurement for operational buying with SAP Ariba for strategic sourcing and collaboration to balance risk and accelerate time-to-value. 

Public sector: SAP Ariba Sourcing and Procurement for Public Sector in S/4HANA (SAP Ariba PPS) is the recommended path for SRM Government Procurement (see SAP Note 3668214). 

SAP is transitioning away from SRM in alignment with its move to S/4HANA and cloud solutions. Mainstream support concludes in 2027, with optional extended maintenance available until 2030 and customer-specific maintenance thereafter. With this timeline in mind, now is the right moment to set a clear transition roadmap. 

 Choose the successor architecture that best fits your goals—S/4HANA for Central Procurement, SAP Ariba, or a hybrid approach (and Ariba PPS for public sector)—and plan a phased migration that accounts for your current release, Unicode posture, integrations, and regulatory needs. Taking these steps early will reduce risk, maintain compliance, and position procurement for modern, innovation-rich capabilities. For official guidance, consult SAP Note 2881788, SAP Note 3668214, and SAP’s S/4HANA maintenance announcement. Incase you have any further queries, please reach out to us at sap_srm_customer_care@sap.com

entry.title: Re: Invoice and item numbers on settlement document flexi...

Add invoice number (VBRK-VBELN) and line item fields (VBRP-POSNR) to that transfer group, then adjust the flexible overview to display them.

entry.title: SoCal Community Event : SAP Business Data Cloud " From Pipelines to Data Products "
entry.content:
The focus of the session was SAP Business Data Cloud  (BDC) and the evolving shift toward data products as a foundation for modern data architectures. Rather than a one-way presentation, the discussion quickly became highly interactive, with participants sharing real-world challenges, perspectives, and use cases.

Together, we:

entry.title: Re: G/L Accounts Mass Change of Company Code Data not pos...
entry.content:
Good day @djwafelap 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your screenshots, this does not look like a restriction in the Manage G/L Account Master Data. You can carry out mass changes in the Chart of Accounts view, in the Company Code View and Controlling Area View.

For the Field Status Group field you mentioned, you can use the mass change function in the company code view to assign field status groups to multiple G/L accounts. See KBA 3633693 and SAP Note 3390424 for more details.

So, in more simple terms:

  • The G/L accounts you selected should appear in the mass change item list.
  • The company code view itself is not the reason why the items are missing.
  • If the same action works in other tenants but not in this one you mentioned, then the issued is most likely related to a UI/session issue, personalization, authorization difference, or a tenant-specific application issue.
  • If you are having the same issue with several users in the same tenant, I recommend raising an SAP case.

However, before raising the incident, I would recommend you check the following first:

  1. Reset the app personalization or your variant in Manage G/L Account Master Data app. Switch back to the standard view, make sure you avoid using the personalized table layout for your first test and run the same mass change again. 
  2. Check whether the issue is specific to you or the user in question. If you ask another user and it works in their case, then the issue is likely related to personalization, cache, or authorization.
  3. Check whether the accounts you selected are really company-code extended. Make use of the Company Code View app and filter by the chart of accounts and company code. In the list, confirm that company code data exists for the one you selected. 
  4. Check the field status group itself. If the target field status group is a standard field status group, do not try to change the standard configuration directly. For a custom field status group, copy the standard field status group in SSCUI 102393 (Define Field Status Variants), save it as a custom Z-entry, and then assign it by mass change in the company code view.  See KBA 3633693and SAP Note 3390424 as mentioned above for more insights.
  5. You can use View Logs and capture technical details.  Since your screenshot shows the View Logs option, open it after reproducing the issue and check whether any backend or UI message is written. If the item list remains empty without a message, open a case.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Requirements Class configuration (OVZH equivalent) in...
entry.content:
Yes, in Public Cloud, those customizing activities are not exposed in CBC. SAP deliberately restricts deep SD/ATP customizing to keep the Clean Core principle intact. So what you can do is

1) Check CBC Activities for ATP

In CBC, look under Sales and Distribution → Basic Functions → Availability Check (aATP). You will find activities for defining checking groups, checking rules, and assigning them to material master / sales document types. The “Requirements Class” level toggle (availability check without transfer of requirements) is not available.

2) Use aATP Features Instead

To achieve your business requirement (don’t reserve stock at order creation, only confirm later via BOP):

  • Configure the checking group so that requirements are not transferred (in Public Cloud this is delivered as standard “No Transfer of Requirements” option).
  • Then rely on Backorder Processing (BOP) to re‑prioritize and confirm quantities closer to delivery dates.

This mimics your idea.  ATP runs at order entry, but stock is not firmly reserved until BOP executes.

3) Alternative Approach

Few clients handle this by using delivery scheduling agreements or delivery proposals rather than reserving stock at order creation. Another option is to use aATP’s Product Allocation or Release for Delivery functionality to control when stock is actually committed.

entry.title: Re: Subtotal on manage sales orders 2 (F3893)
entry.content: The app is designed as a transactional Fiori app focused on order creation, change, and monitoring, not as a reporting tool. SAP deliberately keeps the UI lean, with emphasis on filters, search, and KPIs rather than grouped subtotals. Subtotals by order type (or other fields) are available in analytical apps or embedded analytics reports, not in Manage Sales Orders V2.

entry.title: Re: integration between SD and CPM
entry.content:
In SAP Commercial Project Management (CPM), the integration with Sales Orders is not fully automated in the way you’re describing. The “Create Sales Order” button in the commercial project workspace is essentially a navigation shortcut to VA01 and it does not generate the order in the background. You still need to create the sales order manually and then assign the WBS element for account assignment.

Some workarounds are

1) Preconfigured templates
You can set up sales order templates or use copy functionality to reduce manual effort. This way, the user only adjusts key fields rather than building the order from scratch.

2) Side‑by‑side automation (SAP BTP)
If automation is a must, you can build an extension on SAP Business Technology Platform that:

  • Listens to the project creation event in CPM.
  • Calls the SD API to create a sales order automatically with the WBS assignment.

This keeps Clean Core intact while delivering automation.

3) Project billing integration

CPM integrates with Project Billing and Resource-Related Billing (RRB), which can generate billing requests automatically. But this is downstream (billing), not upstream (sales order creation).

entry.title: Re: Cascading Stock Transfer Between MRP Areas and Two Ce...
entry.content:
Standard MRP cannot handle “WH1 first, then WH2” fallback logic automatically. SPKs and quota arrangements are static; they don’t evaluate stock availability dynamically. To achieve your requirement, you either:

  • Redesign the supply chain model (e.g., treat WH1/WH2 as separate plants with clear rules), or
  • Build a BTP extension to add conditional sourcing logic.

SAP Best Practice in Public Cloud is to keep replenishment rules simple and avoid conditional chains so that the system expects you to model the supply network explicitly rather than embed “if‑then” logic in MRP.

entry.title: Re: Unable to post transaction through post capitalization
entry.content:
Hi @Sandeep46 ,
My friend your issue is caused by a mismatch between capitalization date, posting date, and fiscal‑year rules in SAP Asset Accounting. SAP does not allow a capitalization date in a future fiscal year when the asset’s “put‑to‑use” date lies in a previous fiscal year.

As per the SAP’s rules for Asset Accounting, Capitalization date and posting date must lie in the same fiscal year. If they do not, SAP blocks the posting. When you create an asset with post‑capitalization, SAP expects the capitalization date to reflect the original year the asset should have been capitalized.

If the put‑to‑use date is in a previous fiscal year, but you enter a capitalization date in 2026, SAP interprets this as inconsistent and throws an error.

This aligns with SAP’s documented behavior, “Both dates must lie within the same fiscal year… SAP does not allow shifting the capitalization date to a different fiscal year when postings already exist or when the put‑to‑use date is earlier.”

The SAP’s official guidance for post‑capitalization (ABNAN / Fiori App “Post Post‑Capitalization”) states, Create the asset with Post‑Capitalization flag. Then enter the original capitalization date (the year the asset should have been capitalized). Post the APC in the current open period, while SAP automatically posts accumulated depreciation for prior years. If the asset was put to use in a previous fiscal year (e.g., 2024), the capitalization date must be in that year — not 2026.


The Correct approach (SAP‑compliant) is to create the asset with the original capitalization date (the year the asset was actually put to use). Enable Post‑Capitalization in the asset master. Post the APC using ABNAN or the Fiori app with a current‑period posting date. SAP will automatically calculate and post accumulated depreciation for the prior years.
If the fiscal year is closed (OAAQ), If the asset fiscal year is closed too far back, you may need to reopen the fiscal year in OAAQ (if allowed by your finance team), or Follow SAP Note 2935975, which provides guidance for post‑capitalization when the closed year prevents posting.

Hope this will give you correct approach

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Pending Authorization Request Notifi...
entry.content:
When an S-user submits an authorization request via the Self-Service Authorization Request feature, the request will be forwarded to the account’s administrators (see Important Contacts), who can then approve or reject the request.  

As you are already aware, the email notification does not include any detailed information. To view full details about the authorization requester and the requested authorization(s), please check the Action Required section within User Management.

For more detailed information, please refer to KBA 2891528 - How to use the Self-Service Authorization Request - SAP for Me

If you have any questions or need further assistance, please feel free to contact us. 

Best regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Intelligent Forecast Calculation Methods (SAP B1 HANA)
entry.content:
Triple Exponential Smoothing (TES), also known as Holt-Winters Exponential Smoothing, is a forecasting method that extends exponential smoothing by considering three components: level, trend, and seasonality. It is used for time series forecasting when data exhibits trends and seasonality.

Here's how Triple Exponential Smoothing works:

  1. Level (L)
  • This represents the current average value of the time series data. It's essentially the baseline around which the forecast is made.
  1. Trend (T)
  • This represents the slope or rate of change of the data. It accounts for the increase or decrease in the values over time.
  1. Seasonality (S)
  • This accounts for repeated patterns or cycles in the data, such as seasonal effects that occur at fixed periods (e.g., monthly, quarterly, yearly).

Mathematical Model

Triple Exponential Smoothing uses the following formulas:

  • Level (L_t):

Lt=α⋅(Yt/St−m)+(1−α)⋅(Lt−1+Tt−1)L_t = \alpha \cdot (Y_t / S_{t-m}) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot (L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})Lt​=α⋅(Yt​/St−m​)+(1−α)⋅(Lt−1​+Tt−1​)

  • Trend (T_t):

Tt=β⋅(Lt−Lt−1)+(1−β)⋅Tt−1T_t = \beta \cdot (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) \cdot T_{t-1}Tt​=β⋅(Lt​−Lt−1​)+(1−β)⋅Tt−1​

  • Seasonality (S_t):

St=γ⋅(Yt/Lt)+(1−γ)⋅St−mS_t = \gamma \cdot (Y_t / L_t) + (1 - \gamma) \cdot S_{t-m}St​=γ⋅(Yt​/Lt​)+(1−γ)⋅St−m​

Where:

  • Y_t is the actual value at time t.
  • L_t is the level at time t.
  • T_t is the trend at time t.
  • S_t is the seasonal component at time t.
  • m is the length of the seasonal cycle.
  • α (alpha), β (beta), and γ (gamma) are the smoothing parameters for level, trend, and seasonality, respectively. They determine how much weight is given to each component (level, trend, seasonality).

Forecasting with Triple Exponential Smoothing

The forecast for future values is calculated using the following formula:

Y^t+k=(Lt+k⋅Tt)⋅St+k−m\hat{Y}_{t+k} = (L_t + k \cdot T_t) \cdot S_{t+k-m}Y^t+k​=(Lt​+k⋅Tt​)⋅St+k−m​

Where k is the number of periods ahead for forecasting.

Key Features

  • Level accounts for the current data trend.
  • Trend helps capture the growth or decline in the data over time.
  • Seasonality adjusts for periodic fluctuations, such as monthly or yearly cycles.

Use Cases

Triple Exponential Smoothing is effective when:

  • Data has both seasonal patterns and trends (e.g., sales data, production volumes, temperature variations).
  • You need to forecast future values based on historical data, especially for long-term planning.

Example:

If you have monthly sales data for a company, and the sales show an upward trend with seasonal peaks during the holiday seasons, TES will help you predict future sales by taking into account:

  • The baseline sales (level)
  • The increasing trend over the years
  • The seasonal spikes around holidays.

Tuning the Parameters:

  • The smoothing parameters (α, β, and γ) are typically optimized to minimize the forecasting error (e.g., Mean Absolute Error, RMSE).
  • α controls the smoothing of the level (smoothing factor for the baseline).
  • β controls the smoothing of the trend.
  • γ controls the smoothing of seasonality.

Triple Exponential Smoothing is a powerful method for handling time series data with both trend and seasonal components, helping businesses and analysts make more accurate forecasts.

Linear Regression with Damped Trend and Seasonal Adjustment (LRDTSA) is a forecasting method that combines linear regression with the concept of a damped trend and incorporates seasonal adjustments. This approach is useful when you're dealing with time series data that exhibits both trend and seasonality, but the trend does not grow exponentially; instead, it decays or flattens over time.

Key Components:

  1. Linear Regression:
    • Linear regression models the relationship between a dependent variable (the forecasted value) and one or more independent variables. In the case of time series, it typically involves modeling the trend over time as a linear function.
    • The basic idea is that the future value is related to the past values through a linear equation:

Yt=β0+β1t+ϵtY_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t + \epsilon_tYt​=β0​+β1​t+ϵt​

Where:

      • YtY_tYt​ is the value at time ttt,
      • ttt is time,
      • β0\beta_0β0​ is the intercept,
      • β1\beta_1β1​ is the slope or trend,
      • ϵt\epsilon_tϵt​ is the error term (residual).
  1. Damped Trend:
    • A damped trend modifies the linear trend by gradually reducing the effect of the trend over time. This helps model scenarios where the growth or decline slows down and eventually flattens.
    • The damped trend is represented as:

Tt=ϕt⋅β1tT_t = \phi^t \cdot \beta_1 tTt​=ϕt⋅β1​t

Where:

      • TtT_tTt​ is the damped trend at time ttt,
      • ϕ\phiϕ is the damping factor (between 0 and 1),
      • ttt is time.
    • The damping factor, ϕ\phiϕ, controls the rate at which the trend slows down. A value closer to 1 means the trend decays slowly, while a value closer to 0 causes the trend to flatten more rapidly.
  1. Seasonal Adjustment:
    • Seasonal adjustment is necessary when the data exhibits seasonality—i.e., patterns that repeat over regular intervals (such as monthly, quarterly, or yearly).
    • The seasonal component is typically represented as a periodic function or a set of seasonal indices:

St=YtLt⋅TtS_t = \frac{Y_t}{L_t \cdot T_t}St​=Lt​⋅Tt​Yt​​

Where:

      • StS_tSt​ is the seasonal component at time ttt,
      • YtY_tYt​ is the observed value at time ttt,
      • LtL_tLt​ is the level component (similar to the smoothed series without trend and seasonality),
      • TtT_tTt​ is the damped trend.
    • The seasonal adjustment factor can be incorporated by dividing or multiplying the forecasted value by the seasonal factor.

The Forecasting Model:

The final forecast combining the damped trend and seasonal adjustment can be represented as:

Y^t+k=Lt+ϕk⋅Tt+St+k\hat{Y}_{t+k} = L_t + \phi^k \cdot T_t + S_{t+k}Y^t+k​=Lt​+ϕk⋅Tt​+St+k​

Where:

  • Y^t+k\hat{Y}_{t+k}Y^t+k​ is the forecasted value at time t+kt+kt+k,
  • LtL_tLt​ is the level at time ttt,
  • ϕk\phi^kϕk is the damping factor raised to the power of **bleep**,
  • TtT_tTt​ is the trend at time ttt,
  • St+kS_{t+k}St+k​ is the seasonal component at time t+kt+kt+k.

How It Works:

  1. Level (L_t): This is the baseline of the time series, adjusted over time based on historical data.
  2. Damped Trend (T_t): The trend initially increases or decreases but gradually slows down, allowing for a more realistic long-term forecast that does not project an ever-growing increase.
  3. Seasonality (S_t): Periodic fluctuations are adjusted for, such as higher sales during the holiday season or lower sales during off-peak months.

Example Use Case:

Imagine you are forecasting the monthly sales for a retail store. The sales data shows:

  • Trend: Sales have been increasing, but at a decreasing rate over time (the growth is slowing down).
  • Seasonality: Sales tend to be higher in December due to the holiday season and lower in the summer months.

Using Linear Regression with Damped Trend and Seasonal Adjustment, the forecast will take into account:

  • The slowdown in sales growth (damped trend),
  • The seasonal spikes in December, and
  • The lower sales in summer.

The result will be a forecast that:

  • Adjusts the trend to flatten as time progresses,
  • Reflects seasonal variations in sales.

Advantages of Damped Trend:

  • Realistic Forecasting: The damped trend makes the forecast more realistic when the underlying data shows signs of diminishing growth or decline.
  • Long-Term Forecasting: It prevents forecasts from showing unrealistic growth (like exponential growth), which can be problematic in many business settings.

When to Use:

  • Business Cycle Forecasting: When growth slows down over time, but you still need to account for the trend.
  • Seasonal Products: When there is a clear seasonal pattern in the data.
  • Sales Forecasting: When sales or demand is trending but not growing indefinitely, and you need to incorporate seasonal fluctuations.

Summary:

Linear Regression with Damped Trend and Seasonal Adjustment is a powerful method for forecasting time series data with both trend and seasonality. It effectively combines the simplicity of linear regression with the flexibility of damped trends and seasonal adjustments, making it suitable for real-world applications like sales forecasting, inventory planning, and demand analysis.

entry.title: Re: OAAQ highest physical year need to reduce from 2026 t...
entry.content:
Hi @Debasis_Sankhua ,
My friend, you cannot reverse an AFABN depreciation posting directly once the fiscal year/period is closed. To correct the missing profit center, you must reverse the FI document using OASV (manual reversal for depreciation postings) and then repost depreciation with the correct account assignment after adjusting the asset master or substitution logic.

Please follow below mentioned steps to fix the issue.

1. Check OAAQ to confirm the highest posted fiscal year.
2. Reverse the depreciation FI document using OASV.
3. Fix the profit center in:
(a) Asset master (AS02), or
(b) Substitution logic, or
(c) Account assignment (AO90), or
(d) Field status of depreciation accounts.
4. Repost depreciation using AFABN (repeat run).
5. If needed, adjust OAAQ after ensuring no postings exist in 2026.

Hope this will help to fix the issue, please don't forget to accept the solution.

entry.title: Week 4: AI Developer Challenge SAP-RPT-1 Model
entry.content:
Week 4: Learn and experience SAP-RPT-1 model


Welcome to week 4 of April's Developer Challenge on AI! Over the past three weeks you've built up a solid toolkit; grounding with RAG, autonomous agents, and tool-equipped reasoning. This week takes a different turn: instead of working with large language models, you'll explore SAP-RPT-1, a foundation model purpose-built for structured, tabular enterprise data.

While LLMs excel at understanding and generating text, many real-world business problems revolve around tables and rows like sales forecasts, churn predictions, anomaly detection. RPT-1 is designed exactly for those use cases, letting you get predictive insights from your enterprise data without the overhead of traditional ML pipelines.

What You'll Achieve


By working through this exercise, you'll learn how to:

- Understand RPT-1's approach: See how a transformer model can be applied to structured/tabular data for classification and regression tasks
- Use the RPT-1 Playground: Explore the model interactively with example datasets to build an intuition for how it works
- Prepare and upload data: Format your own dataset as JSON and submit it to the model for inference
- Interpret predictions: Read and evaluate the model's output for both classification and regression scenarios

What is RPT-1?


SAP-RPT-1 SAP's Relational Pretrained Transformer model is a foundation model trained on structured data. It is available in Generative AI Hub to gain predictive insights from enterprise data. The model works by uploading example data rows as JSON and can do classification and regression predictions on your dataset.

The RPT-1 Playground


👉 Familiarize yourself with the SAP-RPT-1 model by reading the paper: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.10707

👉 Open the SAP-RPT-1 Playground. Use one of the example files from the playground to understand how the model works.

The challenge


Now that you are familiar with SAP-RPT-1 and how to use the playground, we want to challenge you.
In the GitHub repository, you can find a CSV file sales.csv. This CSV file includes sales data for different types of products. The goal is to use SAP-RPT-1 to predict the missing values for some of these products. The model should predict the sales group respectively for the product type. If you run the prediction through SAP-RPT-1 you will see, initially, that it is hallucinating.

👉 Make sure you work with the SAP-RPT-1 model in a way that these predictions come out correct.

You can use the SAP-RPT-1 Playground to upload the CSV and test your predictions and trainings.

To participate: Post which fields you have changed in order to make the predictions correct.

entry.title: How to find missing SQL indexes and to create them in SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

When you're occurring performance issue, you should first seek for missing SQL indexes. Indeed, when SQL index is missing, your database needs to run a full scan on the table (more CPU consumption, and less CPU for the other queries) and SQL query response is slowing down by this heavy processing.

Analys SQL Index

Check SQL index existence

In HAC, you can easily check SQL index existence for each table: /hac/azurehac/schema/

hac/azurehac/schema/tables/<table>

Find missing SQL index

In HAC, you can select time window where performance issue occurred for retrieving heaviest SQL queries. Then, you can display Query Plan where missing SQL indexes could be identified.

/hac/azurehac/query/

It can happen that longest query log is not available: "Query Store is not properly configured". In this case, you should create SAP support ticket to re-activate it.

ALTER DATABASE CURRENT SET QUERY_STORE CLEAR;

Create SQL Index

From items.xml (recommended)

When it is possible, you should create SQL index in items.xml. There's several advantages for:

  • Index will be created at each migrate data deployment. You don't have to manually create this missing index for each env.
  • Index won't be overwritten or removed during migrate data deployment.

However, items.xml doesn't allow you to create index in table relation. For this specific case, you need to create manually the index (see next chapter).

SQL index creation example with inclusion

CREATE NONCLUSTERED INDEX [<Name of Missing Index, sysname,>] 
ON [dbo].[products] ([TypePkString],[p_catalogversion]
INCLUDE ([p_baseproduct])
<indexes>
  <index name="productIDX">
   <key attribute="catalogversion"/>
   <key attribute="itemtype"/>
   <include attribute="baseproduct"/>
  </index>
</indexes>

Note that attribute TypePkString is named itemtype in items.xml

From HAC

When you need to create manually SQL index from HAC, you must name it by using following pattern defined in Managed properties. Otherwise, your index will be dropped on the next migrate data deployment.

bootstrap.init.type.system.custom.index.ignore.names.starting.with=nci_wi_, idx_*dba, nci_msft_

There's two case which is justified to create manually SQL index :

  • For the relation table
  • In runtime emergency situation as the live production system

In HAC, it is possible to use Groovy for this index creation. Pay attention, index creation, in large table, can generate heavy workload on database system. It is recommended to execute them only during low traffic period.

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003508357

import de.hybris.platform.core.Registry

conn = Registry.getCurrentTenant().getDataSource().getConnection()
stmt = conn.createStatement()
ddlQuery = "YOUR CREATE INDEX SQL COMMAND"
stmt.executeUpdate(ddlQuery)

From SAP Support

Finally, you can also ask DBA support for this SQL index creation by creation SAP support ticket in https://me.sap.com/app/casecreate

Conclusion

As we can see, identify missing SQL indexes and to create them is easy task. Nevertheless, you should keep in mind that SQL index creation can lead to few performance degradation in write access and it will increase your database storage usage. 

In term of cost, database CPU usage is larger more expensive than database storage. And eCommerce solution is mostly read access system for critical business operation (frontend activity). By consequence, when SQL index is missing and it impacts your performance, you should probably need to create it.

Table size is another aspect that should be considered. Over 1 million entry, SQL index starts to become less efficient. And you should do some data cleaning operation and/or redesign your data model by introduction deployment table (additional technical table behind logical SAP Commerce Cloud table).

If you've doubt or if you're encountering some difficulty, you should not hesitate to contact direct SAP Expert Service :  sapcx-expertservices@sap.com

entry.title: Reward & Recognition : Configure business rule to avoid "No budget holder" issue for top hierarchy
entry.content:
When using "Nominator" budget hierarchy, the top of hierarchy of company(without manager) cannot nominate others due to system validation.

So we can use the business rule configure in the save rule of spot award program to switch the budget holder of top of hierarchy to achieve the scenario.

Prerequisite:

Switch the program's budget hierarchy as "Nominee". (Since the system will validate the budget holder at the first time, the user do not have manager cannot identify another user as its budget holder, so we will use the business rule to identify the correct budget holder later.)

Configure Step:

  1. Create a business rule as below, replace the Mohan Kumar as your top of hierarchy and Program as your program.
  2. Go to Configure Object Definitions -> Object Definitions -> Spot Award
  3. Scroll down, add the business rule created in Step1 to the section Save Rules.
  4. Then the program will achieve below scenario:
    • If the nominator is not the top of hierarchy , then set the budget holder as nominator's manager.
    • If the nominator is the top of hierarchy , then set the budget holder as nominator.

Additional:

You can also add the user without manager but still need to use spot award to nominate others to the rule condition list.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2604
entry.content:
Before we get into this month's update, make sure you’re registered for the upcoming What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2605 webcast, covering the top features from the releases 2603-2605. Alongside the blogs, it gives you live demos and the chance to ask your questions as you go. We’ll be answering them live via chat during the session. Register here.

Asset Reliability Engineering

See what changed (and why) in your recommendations  - New Change Log

When recommendations evolve over time, one critical question often remains unanswered:
What changed, when, and who changed it?

New Change Log tab page in the Manage Recommendations application

The Challenge

Changes to recommendations used to be either overwritten or require manual duplication. This created a gap:

  • No clear audit trail
  • Limited transparency into decision-making
  • Loss of historical context

For reliability engineers and maintenance planners, this made it difficult to track how and why recommendations have evolved, especially in environments where traceability is essential.

The Solution

We introduced a change log/timeline tab page for Recommendations in Manage Recommendations and Implement Recommendations applications.

All changes to recommendation business objects are automatically documented - independent of versioning - and made visible directly in the UI through a dedicated timeline view.

Behind the scenes, change logging is already captured at database level by the SAP Cloud Application Programming framework. This enhancement brings that data to life by exposing it to users in a clear, accessible way.

The Outcome

With the new change log/timeline:

  • Every change is visible
  • Every action is traceable
  • Every decision has context

This enables:

  • Greater transparency across teams
  • Improved auditability and compliance
  • Better-informed decision-making over time

For more information, refer to View and Export Change Logs .

 

Revising a Standalone Recommendation

Maintenance strategies don’t stay right forever. Assets change. Conditions change. Insights improve.
But if every change means overwriting the past, you lose the very context that explains why decisions were made.

And that’s a real issue:
Without history, there’s no true learning, no auditability, and no confidence in how your strategy evolves.

The Challenge

Today, updating a recommendation forces a compromise:

  • Overwrite it and lose historical context
  • Recreate it and break continuity

Over time, this leads to fragmented strategies and limited traceability especially across long asset lifecycles and changing teams.

The Solution

The new Revise button for Standalone Recommendations allows Reliability Engineers to:

  • Create a new version of an implemented recommendation
  • Carry forward all relevant data automatically
  • Track changes between versions with full transparency

New 'Revise' button for an implemented recommendation in the 'Manage Recommendations' application

Instead of disconnected updates, recommendations now evolve as a continuous, structured history.

The Outcome

  • End-to-end traceability of strategy changes
  • Clear audit trail across versions
  • Confidence to adapt without losing context
  • Continuity over decades of asset operation

In a nutshell, change your strategy - without losing your story.

For more information, see Revising a Standalone Recommendation.

 

Consolidation of Recommendations

The Challenge

As recommendations grow across assets, assessments, and teams, a new challenge emerges:
too many similar recommendations, scattered across the system.

Even when they relate to the same issue, you're left:

  • Reviewing and implementing them individually
  • Duplicating effort across similar entries
  • Losing efficiency in planning and execution

Over time, this doesn't just slow things down - it creates unnecessary complexity in your maintenance processes.

With the Consolidate Recommendations feature, you can now merge multiple standalone recommendations into a single, unified entry.

This allows you to bring together recommendations that share common characteristics - such as the same type, subtype, or failure data profile - and manage them as one.

Instead of handling multiple similar recommendations separately, you streamline them into a single action.

The Solution

When consolidating recommendations, the system ensures consistency by applying a few key rules:

  • You can select up to 10 recommendations for consolidation
  • Only recommendations in the following statuses can be selected:
    • Created
    • In Process
    • Ready for Release
    • In Review
  • All selected recommendations must share:
    • The same Type and Subtype
    • The same Failure Data Profile
    • The same Planning Plant and Maintenance Plant
  • For assessment-based recommendations:
    • The Source Assessment must be identical

These checks ensure that only truly comparable recommendations are merged - maintaining data integrity while reducing duplication.

The Outcome

By consolidating recommendations, you:

  • Reduce duplication across similar actions
  • Simplify implementation workflows
  • Improve clarity for planners and engineers
  • Focus effort where it matters most

Instead of managing many similar recommendations, you move forward with one clear, consolidated action. This is a small change with a big impact - helping you keep recommendation management lean, structured, and scalable.

 

Asset Health Monitoring

Interacting with Embedded IoT Platform via Command Line Interface

SAP Asset Performance Management now supports the Command Line Interface of the Embedded IoT platform provider. As a Data Integration engineer, you can use this interface to efficiently work with the platform and automate tasks such as device management. This improves operational efficiency and enables scripted automation of routine tasks.

For more information, refer to Interacting with Embedded IoT Platform via Command Line Interface.

 

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • Don't forget to register for the next webcast What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2605 webcast, covering the top features from the releases 2603-2605. Register here.
  • Save the date for our next roadmap webcast on June 3, 4pm CET. Registration will be open soon.
  • Check the latest update of the APM link collection. 
  • You can explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.

entry.title: SAP Cloud ALM Questions
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

I have some questions,

Are there email notifications in defects? I mean when We change status to New, In Process, Retest Required, is possible to send notifications to Responsible to Defect and to Key User who have created the defect?

I just achieve that, changing status and adding a new comment with mention @…….. this was the only way to send eMail notifications to involved people

Another thing is, 

As a Tester you can see all Test Cases in SAP Cloud ALM, how can We restrict the access? I mean that the Key User just watch the Test Cases assigned to him

Thanks for your help


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud ALM Questions
entry.content:
Dear customer,

to answer your first question:

SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation apps have "in-app" notifications for specific activities (e.g. a Defect is assigned to your user). It is possible to enable a very basic e-mail notification that will be sent at the same time that the in-app notifications are created.

Please see 3390449 - E-mail notifications for SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation applications

 

In regards to your qestion how to restrict the access to test cases, we recommend combining role collections to enable relevant authorizations for your tasks. For specific requirements, you can create your own custom role collections.

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/role-collection-recommendations?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

Please also refer to 3499177 - Restricting user actions in SAP Cloud ALM for Implementation Features

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: How to handle subcontracting procurement between group companies?
entry.content:
There are two companies, A and B, both under the same group. Company A is a trading company. A receives returned products that need repair, but A does not have the repair capability and needs to hand them over to B for repair. During the repair process, raw materials are required. The raw materials are at Company B, and A does not provide them. Company B also needs to use labor. The raw material and labor costs consumed by B during the repair process need to be borne by Company A.

My current idea is for Company A to issue a subcontracting purchase order, but I am not sure if there is a better solution. May I ask whether any engineer has designed a similar scenario?

entry.title: Asset Acquistion report S_ALR_8701205
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are preparing monthly CAPEX reporting and are using the standard Asset Accounting report S_ALR_87012050.

When the report is executed without ALV Grid, the field “Ordinary Planned Depreciation” is displayed correctly. However, when we switch to ALV Grid, this field is not available—it does not appear in the ALV output nor in the layout/field selection.

Has anyone faced this behavior before?
Is there a standard way to extract or display “Ordinary Planned Depreciation” in ALV Grid for report S_ALR_87012050, or is this a known SAP limitation?

Any guidance or clarification would be greatly appreciated.

Nag

entry.title: Agent Explainability as a Service: A New Paradigm for Enterprise AI
entry.content:
Why Enterprise AI Has an Explainability Problem

As AI agents become more deeply embedded in enterprise software, there is a growing tension between capability and trust. Modern agentic systems powered by foundation models are highly capable, yet notoriously opaque. When an AI agent recommends a procurement action, flags a return request, or proposes a correction to master data, the natural question from any reasonable user is: why? The answer, all too often, is simply not available.

Explainable AI (XAI) is not a new field. Techniques for attributing model outputs to input features, measuring prediction confidence, and assessing fairness across demographic groups have matured considerably over the past decades. Methods for local and global explanation, counterfactual reasoning, and retrieval provenance are well established in extant literature. And yet, in practice, enterprise development teams implementing AI agents are building explanation pipelines from scratch, project by project, with no shared infrastructure and no consistent output format. The result is duplicated effort, inconsistent metrics, and explanations that are often either too technical for business users or too vague to be useful to developers.

The problem, in other words, is not the absence of XAI techniques. It is the absence of a service layer that makes those techniques accessible in a way that is standardized, scalable, and audience-aware.

 

Explainability as a Service via MCP

One architectural approach to address this gap is to offer XAI functionality as a service, delivered through an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server. MCP is an open protocol that allows AI agents to interact with external tools and services through a uniform interface. Rather than embedding explanation logic within each AI agent, the agent offloads this responsibility to a dedicated explainability service. The agent sends a request, and the service returns governed explanation artifacts such as feature attributions, confidence reports, fairness assessments, data provenance records, and natural language narratives, all conforming to well-defined contract schemas. These schemas can be expressed in structured formats such as JSON, providing a stable, model-agnostic interface that any compliant agent can consume regardless of the underlying model type or vendor.

What makes this approach novel and useful is not the application of any single XAI technique, but the combination of design decisions that address the real-world complexity of enterprise AI deployment. First, the service is audience-aware. A business user asking why an order was flagged needs a concise narrative that connects the explanation to their domain context. A developer diagnosing unexpected behavior needs raw attribution values, diagnostic plots, and audit metadata. A single underlying computation should be able to produce both, and the service layer is an ideal place to enforce this differentiation. Second, the service is adaptive: it selects the appropriate explanation method based on metadata about the model, such as its origin, type, and registered policy profile, rather than requiring the calling agent to specify or know this detail. Third, the service enforces governance: explanations are reproducible, fairness gaps are assessed against configurable thresholds, exception conditions are diagnosed and reported systematically, and every artifact is logged for audit purposes.

Taken together, these properties turn explainability from an afterthought into a first-class feature of any AI agent that integrates with the service. The decoupling of explanation logic from agent codebases means that teams working on different applications can share the same explanation infrastructure. Consistency across projects becomes achievable without coordination overhead. New XAI methods can be introduced centrally and made immediately available to all consuming agents.

 

Implications and Outlook

The enterprise implications are significant. Organizations deploying AI in regulated or high-stakes contexts face growing pressure from regulators, auditors, and internal governance functions to demonstrate that automated decisions are fair, traceable, and contestable. An explainability service of the kind described above directly addresses this pressure, not by adding compliance overhead but by making compliance a natural byproduct of the system architecture.

At the time of writing, SAP is developing an MCP-based XAI as a Service offering, hosted on SAP Business Technology Platform, that will bring standardized, governed, and audience-aware explainability to AI agents across the SAP ecosystem. Stay tuned for more.

 

Additional co-authors and contributors: Amrit Nandan, Yugandhar M.

entry.title: Re: SAC Data Action for Allocation
entry.content:
@Aastha30 

I would advise to write this in blocks or stages. First process your drivers. If they do change frequently you could separate this out from main logic to prevent it eating your execution time in every run.

Now for the main Allocation logic, scope on pre allocated data and drivers you processed in previous step.

You can structure it as below.

IF[ALLOC_CYCLE].[p/Active]="Y"   THEN // Filter out only Active Allocation cycles.

         IF [ALLOC_CYCLE].[p/Active]="1" and [ALLOC_CYCLE] !=[ALLOC_CYCLE] THEN // Repeat this for all stages in the sequence 1,2 ..

..... Your allocation multiplication here

ENDIF

ENDIF

Hope this helps !

Nikhil

entry.title: Deactivate the use of Reference Exchange Rate Indicator in Group Reporting
entry.content:
Hello, 

we are setting up the Group Reporting solution for our client. We use the Translation Method S0905. The Reference Exchange Rate Indicator used is C. 

Regarding translation of Local Currencies to the Group Currency, we want the B/S items to be translated using the CLOsing Exchange Rate, and the P/L items via the AVERage Exchange Rate. 

PROBLEM: When running the Currency Translation task within the Data Monitor app, we receive CTA (Currency Translation Adjustments) differences created for P/L FS items (posted to the 314800 FS Item). It is because the Reference ER is based on the CLOsing ER and the P/L FS items are based on the Average ER. 

This problem does not occur for B/S Items because it is based on the CLOsing ER (like the Reference ER). We want to achieve the same thing with the P/L items. 

Our client does not need to use the Reference Exchange Rate. This means, we don't want any CTA to be created neither for B/S nor P/L FS items. Is there any way to deactivate the Reference ER functionality? 

Or is the special FS Item 314800 can be hidden from the reporting? 

Many thanks in advance!! I will reward kudos to helpful solutions

entry.title: Re: Asset Acquistion report S_ALR_8701205
entry.content:
Hi 

Yes, this is a known technical limitation of the report structure for S_ALR_87012050

For CAPEX and depreciation reporting in ALV, S_ALR_87011963 is generally more flexible.

Ensure you use a Sort Variant that includes the "Depreciation" totals. In the selection screen, check the box for "List assets" and select the "ALV Grid" radio button. This report typically handles the field catalog for depreciation values better than the Asset History Sheet (87012050).

entry.title: Postal Payment with Third Party (Outgoing Payments) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
This blog post introduces a special payment method - postal money order in Slovakia and Czech Republic for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

When a payer wants to pay their payee through the post office, they first pay at the post office, and the payee can get the payment through this postal service.

In this blog post the focus is on the business scenarios with postal services in Czech Republic and Slovakia. When the payment got paid out to the payee. You generate a payment file for the outgoing payment using the payment format.

For the specific payment formats for the postal services:

  • Czech Republic - CZ_PSTLMONEYORDER_B
  • Slovakia -  SK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E

Prerequisite

Business Add-Ins
In the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, based on your business requirements, you can implement the following business add-ins (BAdIs):

  • BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_CALCULATE_C: For calculating the payment fee, for postal money orders.
  • BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_POST_ENBL_C: To enable the posting of an additional document that includes both the base amount and the charge amount.
  • BADI_FKK_PAYM_CHRG_POST_PREP_C: For preparing the necessary information for posting a document that includes the base amount and the charge amount.

Note: To post additional documents with a total amount for payments and postal charges, you need to assign the business partner and the contract account. This master data is determined via External Identification in the BAdI implementation. Based on external identification - the Business Partner/Contract Account and assigned Bank details of Czech/Slovakian post offices are used in the documents

Bank Account Settings
Since the payments with money order are not directly paid out from the payer's bank account, instead it is paid out from the post office, it is necessary to create a fictive bank account and account ID for the post office.

Open the Manage Bank Accounts app.
For your company code, create a fictive Bank Account and Account ID for the Slovak post.


Define Payment Methods
Payments to payees through the post office essentially is processing payment. Therefore, you must select a payment method and assign it to the payment medium format.

  1. Open the Define Payment Methods configuration activity (ID: 101952) in the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Select the pre-delivered payment formats or use your own configured formats.
    Sample values:
    Payment MethodPayment Mediums Format
    P - Money order ESK_PSTLMONEYORDER_E
    P - Money order ECZ_PSTLMONEYORDER_B
  3. Assign Third-Party Postal Payment as the payment medium format.

Note to Payee
You can maintain the Note to payee for the payment medium in the Maintain Note to Payee Type for Payment Medium (ID: 103949) configuration.

Define Parameters for Paying Out

For Postal Money Order B, you maintain sender identification, for example a six-digit numeric value that uniquely identifies the sender. The number is assigned to the sender by the post when the agreement is settled.

Fill in the Customer number in the Define Parameters for Foreign Payment Transactions (ID: 101953) configuration.


Procedure of Generating Payment with Postal Money Orders

  1. Set up the master data of business partner with payee's address only (payee's bank information isn't required). The Slovak Post finds the payees with their address to pay out.
    Set up also the master data for the post office as the following example:
  2. Post a payable item that you need to pay to the payee.
  3. Execute a payment run and clear the payable documents. The total amount of the contract accounting document is posted. You can see the information in the Log details as follows.
  4. In the next phase, the following actions are required for the two entities involved.
    • For your house bank: Run a payment run and generate a payment file for the bank. Your bank then transfers money to the account of the post office.
      Note: This condition serves as a prerequisite for the post office to print out the pay slips for the payee.
    • For the post office: Generate a payment medium file with the necessary information that is sent to the post office. If the money arrives in the post office's bank account, the post office prints out the pay slip and pay to the payee.

Is this blog post helpful to you? Leave comments below to let us know your thoughts.

entry.title: Demo Scenario: Clean Core Extensibility with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build
entry.content:
Co-Authors: @Sivakumar_Subramaniam , @ShivaniShankar 

A common feedback when talking about clean core extensibility is the absence of demo-ready content to visualize these guidelines that have evolved over time. This blog post attempts to take an extensibility scenario as example and showcase the equivalent scenario in a clean-core compliant world. Nevertheless, we cannot claim to be covering every possible flow in one post. The intent is to showcase one example covering a wide range. In the process, we walk through some of the decisions taken that go beyond technology and consider business aspects as well. A lot of technical details are omitted since this post is already quite long. Look forward to the discussions and feedback in the comments section to assess interest and demand for similar posts/more technical versions. 

Recommended Pre-read: 

While an effort is made to abstract technical complexity to a great extent, a reader still needs some basic understanding of the SAP ecosystem, how extensions work, why they exist and some background of clean core.

To maximize takeaways from this blog post, consider reading the clean core extensibility whitepaper before you start, particularly if "Clean Core" sounds unfamiliar. Due to the rich context, answers to why clean core etc available in the whitepaper already, we skip it here. Nevertheless, a lot of what this blog post covers would not make much sense without this background/context. 

Key Assumptions: 

1. We consider level-A of clean core extensibility since this is the highest standard. If one were to target level-B for example, the target architecture might look a little different with potential reuse of certain objects

2. For simplicity and to keep the focus on extensibility, we assume there is no fit-to-standard solution available for this scenario which may or may not be factually correct. In a real project, please consider fit-to-standard analysis and an analysis of your existing custom-developed artefacts before developing something new

Scenario Description : 
- Provide a capability to add custom gift cards when creating sales orders in SAP Cloud ERP Private (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition)

- On applying the gift card, a discount is added to the sales order by an amount equal to the gift card amount

- On creation of a sales order, loyalty points need to get accrued against the membership of the business partner (from the sales order). For simplicity, assume only business partners of type "Customer"

- The loyalty program has tiers and based on the tier the customer is on, he/she/they accrue a percentage of the sales order value as points

- It shall be possible for a customer to request an upgrade to their loyalty program tier. In this case, approval from an admin is required before the change is active 

Scenario Refinement:  

Understanding/context: When working with sales orders, this company has 2 parallel reward programs. One, a gift card that can be applied on creation of the sales order for an immediate discount. Another, a loyalty program where one accrues points with each sales order. Potentially, for redemption (not in scope here) through different means at a later point in time

Tasks:

- Since gift cards need to be applied, there is also an unsaid need to be able to create and maintain these gift cards (by an admin). This means an app to create/maintain gift cards with appropriate fields

- To be able to apply the gift card when creating a sales order, an option is needed directly in the sales order application (or transaction)

- The applied gift card should impact the value of the sales order before creation (not post-processing) 

- Similarly, loyalty program tiers would need to be maintained (possibly as business configuration)

- The loyalty points are calculated based on the created sales order and don't have a dependency or need to update/modify the consistency of the sales order itself. This could be handled as a separate activity from the creation of the sales order

Solution Architecture (Classic Extensibility - non-Clean Core level-A):In the classic extensibility scenario which is non-clean core level-A, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - WebDynPro Application
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in VA01 (Transaction code for sales order creation) - User Exit
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Module Pool Program
  4. Tier upgrade - Classic workflow

 Solution Architecture (with ABAP Cloud and SAP Build - Clean Core)

In the clean-core level-A compliant extensibility scenario, we use the following technology choices to build this - 

  1. Gift Card Maintenance - Application developed using RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) with on-stack developer extensibility. Yes! with real code written by a developer inside the S/4HANA system outside of key user tools and still the highest level of clean core! If this is against your understanding of clean core, read SAP's official whitepaper (section 3.2 at the time this blog post is written) linked in the pre-read section of this blog post!  
  2. Capability to add gift card to sales order in the standard sales order Fiori app  - Key User Extensibility
  3. Loyalty Management Application - Side-by-side extension using BTP ABAP Environment
  4. Maintenance of tiers of the loyalty program - Business Configuration in the BTP ABAP Environment (not shown in the demo except as value help)
  5. Tier upgrade - SAP Build Process Automation
  6. Communication between S/4HANA and BTP - Event Mesh (or Advanced Event Mesh)

Alternate options - The Loyalty Points Application could also have been developed side-by-side using CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) with additional handling for tier maintenance. 

Decision Criteria (Technical and Business): 

  1. The use case around application of gift card has a very tight dependency on the sales order. The standard SAP sales order application needs to understand and bring up the gift cards for a value help, have the gift cards applied consistently across impacted business objects when the sales order is updated. All of this needs to happen as part of the sales order creation/update process in S/4HANA. This makes on-stack developer extensibility and key-user extensibility good candidates. 
  2. The loyalty points application has 2 factors to consider - Side-by-side v/s on-stack and CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
    • Side-by-Side v/s On-Stack:
      • Decision: Side-by-Side
      • In private cloud/on-premise, SAP ships a new major version every 2 years. By moving out of the core (S/4HANA) anything that can be moved logically without adverse performance/over-engineering, etc, the customer can benefit from always being on the latest release and getting the latest updates. This de-risks innovation from upgrade project timelines and upgrade strategy. 
      • Enforcement of clean core - With the side-by-side approach, clean core is enforced with syntax checks etc since ABAP Cloud is the only option on BTP ABAP Environment as opposed to governance based approaches on-stack which may need an ABAP Cloud package and processes to ensure a developer doesn't slip back to the familiar classic extensibility when cloud ready alternatives are available.
    • CAP v/s ABAP Cloud
      • Decision: Both options are valid. Here, ABAP Cloud due to developer skill set and business configuration handling know-how. Since each option has strengths and benefits, refer the BTP Developer's guide for details. To generalize any of the two as always the better option without case-to-case analysis and comparison may be an unfair/sub-optimal decision. 

General question on BTP ABAP Environment cost -
"The cost of BTP ABAP Environment is higher" - Apart from the smaller runtime size now available, which reduces costs, the cost needs to be spread out across applications being developed. One instance of the BTP ABAP Environment can be reused for multiple applications so long as the runtime/memory requirements are within available limits. Compare this to additional costs for CF Runtime (for example) for each deployed app with CAP and the costing at solution level at scale tends to be similar. This is not to say CAP is a bad option, just that when the decision is influenced by cost, how this cost is calculated decides quite a bit! When this becomes equivalent, the models can be compared on best fit for the use case on technical/architectural grounds.

Demo of functional scenario in both approaches along with ATC findings across levels - B, C, D on the package where classic extensibility was used

The following demo showcases the scenario with classic extensibility as well as with Clean Core Level-A. It also touches upon ATC checks on the package containing the classic extension. To understand how ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) helps categorize the findings, refer section 3.3.4(at the time of writing) of the clean core extensibility whitepaper (in the pre-read section)

 

Call to Action: Comment below what you would wish was better covered in the post and what key takeaways you had! Your inputs help us understand and validate where such blog posts can add more value

entry.title: Re: Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation recalculates On‑A...
entry.content:
For advanced Foreign currency Valuation you configure the accounts that you want to valuation 

See  3219200 - How to Post Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation - SAP for Me

 

See Configuration Requirements Checklist for Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation 

..

Assign Semantic Tags to Financial Statement Versions

notes 
"You've assigned the semantic tag FX to the account node or individual accounts in the financial statement version that are valuated when you run the Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation job. This means that these accounts are the source accounts for the valuation. For more information see Assigning Semantic Tags to an FSV."

entry.title: Re: SAP Public cloud - Sales BOM for Service item (TAD)
entry.content:
Service item categories (SERV) are not designed to be part of a sales BOM explosion. The item category determination logic in Public Cloud is simplified and locked down and you cannot freely assign SERV to explode under LUMF.

Some workarounds are

1) Model services differently
Instead of trying to explode a service item in a sales BOM, model the service as a separate sales item (manual entry or template). You can use product bundles or solution orders (if available in your scope) to combine materials and services in one order.

2) Use configurable product bundles (SAP Best Practice)
Public Cloud supports bundling logic where you can sell a package of goods + services. This is the recommended way to handle “service with material” scenarios, rather than forcing BOM explosion.

3) Alternative: Service BOM in Project/Service Management
If the requirement is to plan services together with materials, consider using Project Billing / Service Orders instead of Sales BOM. These processes allow you to combine service and material items in one structure.

entry.title: Posting of Periodic Accrual Amounts
entry.content:
To post accrual amounts at the end of a period, you can use the transaction ACEPOSTINGRUN in the S/4HANA Accrual Engine.

  • This transaction is used to post periodic accruals for all applications that work with the Accrual Engine.
  • On the selection screen, you’ll find the field “Application Component”:
    • If you want to post manual accruals, enter ACAC in this field.
  • The fields Accrual Object and Accrual Subobject depend on the application component:
    • For manual accruals, the Accrual Object is a combination of the accrual object category and the accrual object number.
    • Use the F4 input help to select the correct value for this field.

 

Posting of Accruals: Define Posting Schema

The posting schema is used to define how accrual postings are structured.

The Posting Schema Specifies:

  • How many line items are to be created in an accrual posting
  • Which symbolic G/L accounts are to be used in each line item
  • What line item type is to be assigned to each line

Important Line Item Types:

  • A line item of type 9003 must be included for periodic accrual postings
  • A line item of type 9000 may be posted to any G/L account, typically an expense account

Standard and Custom Schemas:

  • Standard posting schemas such as SAP_ACE_PP are delivered by SAP
  • Custom posting schemas can be created by customers in the IMG activity "Define Posting Schema.

 

 

Posting of Accruals: Assign Posting Schema

  • For each accrual item type that triggers accrual postings, a posting schema must be assigned.
  • This assignment is carried out in the IMG activity:
    Assign Accrual Item Types to Journal Entry Types and Posting Schemas

Posting Schema Assignment Rules:

  • If an accrual item type is configured to perform all types of postings, it must be assigned to three posting schemas, one for each posting type (ACE TType):
    • IP – Opening Posting
    • PP – Periodic Posting
    • FP – Final Posting
  • If only periodic postings are required, the accrual item type must be assigned to one posting schema, for ACE TType = PP.

 

Additional Requirements:

  • Each assigned posting schema must include a line item with type 9003 (required for periodic accruals).
  • In the same IMG activity, the document type to be used in accrual postings is also assigned.

entry.title: Re: How to handle subcontracting procurement between grou...
entry.content:
Options to design this in Public Cloud

1) Subcontracting PO (your idea)

Company A issues a subcontracting PO to Company B for the repair service. B consumes its own raw materials and labor internally. Costs are invoiced back to A via the PO.

Advantage: Simple, standard, supported in Public Cloud.
Limitation: You won’t see the detailed raw material consumption in A’s books — only the repair service cost.

2) Intercompany Service PO

Instead of subcontracting, treat the repair as a service procurement. A issues a PO for a “Repair Service” material/service master. B delivers the service and invoices A. Raw material and labor costs are absorbed in B’s cost accounting, then passed to A via the service price.

Advantage: Clean intercompany process, aligns with Public Cloud best practice.
Limitation: Less visibility into the BOM/raw material detail at A.

3) Intercompany STO + Service Billing

If you want to track the physical flow of goods (returned product moving from A to B), you can use a Stock Transport Order (STO) to send the product to B. B repairs it, consumes raw materials, and then returns the product to A. The repair service is billed separately via an intercompany service invoice.

Advantage: Full visibility of product movement + service cost.
Limitation: More complex (two flows: STO for goods, service PO for repair).

So the recommended Best Practice is

  • Use intercompany service procurement (PO for repair service).
  • If physical product movement must be tracked, combine it with an STO.
  • Subcontracting PO is technically possible, but it is designed for scenarios where the buyer provides components. Since A does not provide raw materials, service procurement is cleaner.

entry.title: Re: Best practices for restricting physical deletion of S...
entry.content:
Dear @Harukiy,

The clean-core way to prevent physical deletion of sales order items is to use the released Cloud Badi SD_SLS_CHECK_DELETE_ITEM. SAP confirms that item deletion is technically prevented by setting the NO_DELETION flag the message is only used to inform the user. SAP also recommends using Reason for Rejection when the sales order should no longer be processed instead of deleting the document

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003152093

The logic should block deletion only when Reason for Rejection is still empty on the sales order item. The relevant item field is:

salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentrjcnreason

Badi implementation example:

IF salesdocument-salesdocumenttype = 'TA'  AND

   salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentrjcnreason IS INITIAL.

  no_deletion = 'X'.

  APPEND VALUE #(

    messagetype = 'I'

    messagetext = |Deletion for item | &

                  |{ salesdocumentitem-salesdocumentitem } | &

                  |not allowed. Please use Reason for Rejection first.|

  ) TO messages.

ENDIF.

The implementation should be created in the Custom Logic app because Public Edition supports released Badi through key-user custom logic

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/using-key-user-in-app-extensibility-to-create-custom-business-logic

Summary:

This solution blocks physical deletion only when the item has no Reason for Rejection. Once the user maintains SalesDocumentRjcnReason the deletion check no longer blocks the action

Best Regards,

Dawid
